tts.instruction: Confident, calm hackathon narration. Native English product names. Slightly slower than chat.

chapters.title: A live subreddit incident should not become five private tabs
chapters.body: Reports, links, queue notes, and chat context collapse into one shared room before duplicate review takes over.
chapters.narration: A subreddit incident does not wait for a meeting. A live sports thread, a game launch, or a finance discussion can turn hostile while moderators are still comparing notes in separate tabs. Incident Room turns that spike into one shared room with evidence, claims, rules, and a bounded AI briefing in the same place.

chapters.title: The app opens inside Reddit as a real Devvit command surface
chapters.body: The judge sees the actual product surface, not only a pitch slide: status, score, candidate room, and moderator actions.
chapters.narration: The app is a Devvit custom post, not a separate dashboard pretending to be a moderation tool. The first screen shows the incident state, score, evidence, and next moderator action where the team already works.

chapters.title: Declare, claim, brief, preview, confirm
chapters.body: This section is recorded from the running React app. It clicks the same flow covered by Playwright.
chapters.narration: Here is the working path. The moderator declares the incident, claims the first high risk evidence item, opens the briefing, previews the action pack, confirms the pack, and checks the after action record. The AI briefing explains the pattern and caveats. It never removes, locks, bans, or mutates Reddit content.

chapters.title: Rules score evidence; Step AI explains what the room is seeing
chapters.body: Watch terms, reports, domains, and account age stay deterministic. Step 3.6 writes the briefing after a moderator opens the room.
chapters.narration: The rules do the scoring because those signals need to be inspectable under pressure. Step three point six then writes a compact incident briefing: likely pattern, recommended action pack, and caveats. The model is useful because it is constrained.

chapters.title: Devvit events, Redis room state, Reddit permissions, and server-side AI
chapters.body: Secrets stay server-side. Timeline, claims, action packs, and after-action metrics persist in Devvit Redis.
chapters.narration: Under the hood, Devvit triggers ingest posts, comments, reports, and mod actions. Redis stores the timeline, claims, briefing state, and after action metrics. The OpenAI compatible StepFun endpoint runs server side with a Devvit secret.

chapters.title: A QR scan does not open a shrunken desktop dashboard
chapters.body: The phone layout keeps the incident state and primary action readable for judges and moderators away from the desk.
chapters.narration: The mobile view matters for judging and for real moderation. A moderator can understand the incident state, briefing, and next review action from a phone scan without hunting through a desktop layout.

chapters.title: Built, tested, uploaded, and submitted for Devvit review
chapters.body: Repo, demo video, Step AI smoke test, Playwright flow, Devvit settings, and review request are all linked in the submission package.
chapters.narration: The repo contains the implementation, tests, bilingual product docs, UI evidence, legal pages, and this repeatable video renderer. Devvit version zero point zero point three has been submitted for review, and the demo video is published as a release asset.

chapters.title: The reviewer can inspect the product, code, video, and moderation boundary
chapters.body: Incident Room is packaged around the exact things a judge can verify: app listing, playtest subreddit, repo, demo, tests, and AI smoke evidence.
chapters.narration: For the final submission, the judge path is straightforward. The Devvit app listing shows the installable mod tool and review status. The playtest subreddit shows the Reddit context. The repository shows the implementation and tests. The video shows the product and the live flow. Most importantly, the moderation boundary is clear: the AI helps the team understand the room, but humans still make the enforcement decision.

chapters.title: Open the room, declare the incident, and watch the handoff stay readable
chapters.body: The first click shows the whole idea: shared context before the incident becomes a private scramble.
chapters.narration: That is the first action worth trying: open the room, declare the incident, and watch the handoff stay readable.
